quiz_questions.question_text: What is terminal velocity?

options.text: The maximum constant speed a falling object reaches
The initial speed of an object when it starts falling
The speed at which an object is first thrown upward
The speed of sound through the air at ground level

quiz_questions.explanation: Terminal velocity is the maximum constant speed reached by a falling object when the upward force of air resistance equals the downward force of gravity. At this point, acceleration becomes zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What two forces are balanced when an object reaches terminal velocity?

options.text: Friction and the applied pushing force
Gravity and air resistance (drag force)
Magnetism and the electric force on it
Tension and the normal contact force

quiz_questions.explanation: Terminal velocity occurs when the downward pull of gravity equals the upward push of air resistance. When these forces balance, the net force is zero and the object stops accelerating.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the acceleration of an object at terminal velocity?

options.text: It equals 9.8 meters per second squared
It is increasing at a steady constant rate
It is zero because the forces are balanced
It is negative and still decreasing rapidly

quiz_questions.explanation: At terminal velocity, gravity and air resistance are equal and opposite, so the net force is zero. According to Newton's second law, if net force is zero, acceleration is also zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: As a skydiver falls, what happens to air resistance before terminal velocity is reached?

options.text: Air resistance stays constant the whole time
Air resistance decreases as speed increases
Air resistance has no effect on the fall
Air resistance increases as speed increases

quiz_questions.explanation: As a falling object speeds up, it pushes through more air per second, so air resistance increases. It keeps increasing until it equals gravity, at which point terminal velocity is reached.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which object would have a lower terminal velocity: a feather or a bowling ball?

options.text: A feather, because it has a large surface area relative to its weight
A bowling ball, because it is much heavier than the feather
Both have the same terminal velocity regardless of properties
Neither reaches terminal velocity when falling in the air

quiz_questions.explanation: A feather has a large surface area compared to its weight, so air resistance balances gravity at a much lower speed. A bowling ball is heavy and compact, so it must fall much faster before air resistance can balance gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does opening a parachute affect terminal velocity?

options.text: It increases terminal velocity significantly
It has no effect on terminal velocity at all
It greatly decreases terminal velocity
It eliminates gravity on the skydiver

quiz_questions.explanation: A parachute greatly increases the surface area catching air, which increases air resistance. This means air resistance balances gravity at a much lower speed, greatly decreasing the terminal velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does a heavier object generally have a higher or lower terminal velocity than a lighter one of the same shape?

options.text: Lower, because heavier objects fall more slowly
Higher, because more air resistance is needed to balance gravity
The same, because weight does not affect terminal velocity
Neither, because heavier objects cannot reach terminal velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: A heavier object has a greater gravitational force pulling it down. Air resistance must match this larger force, which only happens at higher speeds. So a heavier object of the same shape has a higher terminal velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to a falling object's speed after it reaches terminal velocity?

options.text: It continues to increase without any limit
It decreases slowly until the object stops
It remains constant and does not change
It oscillates between fast and slow speeds

quiz_questions.explanation: Once terminal velocity is reached, the forces are balanced and acceleration is zero. The object continues falling at the same constant speed. It does not speed up or slow down.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which factor does NOT affect an object's terminal velocity?

options.text: The object's surface area facing the wind
The object's weight or gravitational force
The color of the object that is falling
The density of the air the object falls through

quiz_questions.explanation: Terminal velocity depends on weight, surface area, shape, and air density. The color of the object has no effect on the forces of gravity or air resistance, so it does not affect terminal velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: When a skydiver first jumps from a plane, is the acceleration closer to 9.8 m/s squared or 0 m/s squared?

options.text: The acceleration is zero from the very start
The acceleration starts close to 9.8 m/s squared
The acceleration is exactly 4.9 m/s squared
The acceleration starts at negative 9.8 m/s sq

quiz_questions.explanation: When first jumping, the skydiver has very low speed, so air resistance is minimal. Gravity dominates, making acceleration close to 9.8 m/s squared. As speed increases, air resistance grows and acceleration decreases toward zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is terminal velocity?

options.text: The constant speed where air resistance equals gravity during a fall
The speed at which an object bounces back upward after hitting ground
The maximum speed an object can ever reach in any situation at all
The initial velocity when an object first starts falling from a height

quiz_questions.explanation: Terminal velocity is the constant speed a falling object reaches when the upward air resistance force exactly equals the downward gravitational force. At this point, the net force and acceleration are both zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'air resistance' mean?

options.text: The force of gravity pulling objects downward
The friction-like force air exerts on moving objects
The weight of the air above an object on Earth
The amount of oxygen available for breathing

quiz_questions.explanation: Air resistance (also called drag) is the force that air exerts on a moving object, opposing its motion. It acts like friction and increases with the speed and surface area of the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'drag force' mean?

options.text: The force that pushes an object forward
The resistance force opposing motion through a fluid
The gravitational pull between two large masses
The elastic force from a compressed spring

quiz_questions.explanation: Drag force is the resistance force that opposes an object's motion through a fluid such as air or water. It depends on the object's speed, shape, size, and the density of the fluid.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'net force' mean in the context of terminal velocity?

options.text: The total combined force after adding all forces together
Only the force of gravity acting on the falling object
Only the air resistance acting on the falling object
The mass of the object multiplied by its total volume

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force is the vector sum of all forces acting on an object. At terminal velocity, the net force is zero because gravity (downward) and air resistance (upward) are equal and opposite.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'surface area' mean in relation to terminal velocity?

options.text: The total outside area of an object exposed to the air
The volume of air inside a hollow falling object
The weight of the object in newtons or pounds
The speed at which the object is currently falling

quiz_questions.explanation: Surface area is the total outside area of an object. A larger surface area catches more air, creating greater air resistance. This is why a parachute (large surface area) slows a skydiver down.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'free fall' mean?

options.text: Falling with only gravity acting, with no air resistance
Falling at terminal velocity through the atmosphere
Falling and then bouncing back up off the ground
Falling very slowly through a thick liquid material

quiz_questions.explanation: Free fall is when an object falls under the influence of gravity alone, with no air resistance. In true free fall (like in a vacuum), all objects accelerate at the same rate regardless of mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'balanced forces' at terminal velocity?

options.text: Forces that are equal in size but opposite in direction
Forces that push the object in the same single direction
Forces that change the acceleration of the object
Forces that exist only when the object is at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Balanced forces are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, resulting in a net force of zero. At terminal velocity, gravity pulling down and air resistance pushing up are balanced forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'fluid density' mean in the context of terminal velocity?

options.text: The speed at which a fluid flows past the object
How tightly packed the particles of a fluid are
The color and temperature of the surrounding fluid
The total amount of fluid in a given container

quiz_questions.explanation: Fluid density is the mass per unit volume of the fluid. Denser fluids (like water versus air) create more resistance, which means terminal velocity is lower in denser fluids.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'streamlined' mean in relation to air resistance?

options.text: Having a shape designed to reduce air resistance
Having a very large and flat surface area
Being painted a bright color for high visibility
Moving at the slowest possible speed always

quiz_questions.explanation: A streamlined shape is smooth and tapered to allow air to flow around it easily, reducing drag. Race cars, airplanes, and birds have streamlined shapes to minimize air resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cross-sectional area' mean for a falling object?

options.text: The total weight of the object during the fall
The area of the object as seen from the direction of motion
The volume of the entire falling object in cubic meters
The distance the object has fallen from its start

quiz_questions.explanation: Cross-sectional area is the area of the object as seen from the direction it is moving. A larger cross-sectional area means more air is pushed aside, creating greater drag and a lower terminal velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A skydiver falls with arms and legs spread out, then tucks into a ball. What happens to their terminal velocity?

options.text: It stays exactly the same in both positions
It increases because the surface area decreased
It decreases because the person is now smaller
The skydiver immediately stops falling down

quiz_questions.explanation: Tucking into a ball reduces the cross-sectional area facing the air. Less surface area means less air resistance, so the skydiver must fall faster before air resistance can balance gravity. Terminal velocity increases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does a flat piece of paper fall more slowly than a crumpled paper ball of the same mass?

options.text: The flat paper has more air resistance due to larger surface area
The crumpled ball is heavier than the flat sheet of paper
Gravity pulls harder on flat objects than on round objects
The flat paper has less mass than the crumpled paper ball

quiz_questions.explanation: The flat paper has a much larger surface area than the crumpled ball. This creates more air resistance, so it reaches terminal velocity at a much lower speed. Both have the same mass, so gravity is the same.

quiz_questions.question_text: A raindrop falls from a cloud. Why doesn't it keep accelerating all the way to the ground?

options.text: Gravity gets weaker as the raindrop falls lower
The raindrop runs out of energy during its fall
Air resistance increases until it equals gravity
The cloud continues to pull it back up slowly

quiz_questions.explanation: As the raindrop speeds up, air resistance increases. Eventually, air resistance equals the gravitational force, and the raindrop reaches terminal velocity. It then falls at a constant speed for the rest of the way down.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut drops a hammer and a feather on the Moon (no atmosphere). What happens?

options.text: The feather floats upward away from surface
The hammer falls faster than the feather
Neither object falls because there is no gravity
Both hit the ground at exactly the same time

quiz_questions.explanation: With no atmosphere, there is no air resistance. Without air resistance, there is no terminal velocity difference. Both objects accelerate at the same rate due to gravity alone and hit the ground at the same time.

quiz_questions.question_text: A skydiver weighing 80 kg and one weighing 60 kg jump together with identical parachutes. Who has the higher terminal velocity?

options.text: The 80 kg skydiver has the higher terminal velocity
The 60 kg skydiver has the higher terminal velocity
Both have exactly the same terminal velocity here
Neither reaches terminal velocity with parachutes

quiz_questions.explanation: With identical parachutes (same surface area), the heavier skydiver has more gravitational force pulling down. Air resistance must be greater to balance it, which happens at a higher speed. So the 80 kg skydiver has higher terminal velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball bearing falls through honey instead of air. How does its terminal velocity compare to falling through air?

options.text: It is the same in both honey and air
It is much lower in honey than in air
It is much higher in honey than in air
It cannot reach terminal velocity in honey

quiz_questions.explanation: Honey is much denser and more viscous than air, creating much greater resistance. The drag force balances gravity at a much lower speed, so terminal velocity in honey is much lower than in air.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coffee filter is dropped and quickly reaches terminal velocity. If you stack two coffee filters together, what happens?

options.text: Terminal velocity stays the same as one filter
The stacked filters never reach terminal velocity
Terminal velocity decreases with two filters
Terminal velocity increases because of more weight

quiz_questions.explanation: Two stacked filters have about the same surface area as one but twice the weight. More gravitational force means air resistance must be greater to balance it, which only happens at a higher speed. Terminal velocity increases.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car designer wants to reduce air resistance on a vehicle. Which body shape would be most effective?

options.text: A flat, boxy shape like a large brick wall
A wide shape with many right angles overall
A smooth, tapered streamlined teardrop shape
A tall rectangular shape that stands upright

quiz_questions.explanation: A smooth, tapered streamlined shape allows air to flow around the vehicle with minimal resistance. This reduces drag, improves fuel efficiency, and allows the car to reach higher speeds with less energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hailstone grows larger as it falls through a storm cloud. How does this affect its terminal velocity?

options.text: Terminal velocity decreases because it is bigger
Terminal velocity increases because it is heavier
Terminal velocity stays exactly the same always
The hailstone stops falling as it grows larger

quiz_questions.explanation: As the hailstone grows, its mass and weight increase faster than its surface area. The increased gravitational force means it needs to fall faster for air resistance to balance gravity, raising its terminal velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a science experiment, a student drops a ping-pong ball and a golf ball from the same height. The golf ball hits the ground first. Why?

options.text: The golf ball has a higher terminal velocity due to greater weight
Gravity pulls harder on the golf ball because it is white
The ping-pong ball is not affected by gravity at all here
The golf ball creates more air resistance than the ping-pong ball

quiz_questions.explanation: The golf ball is much heavier than the ping-pong ball but similar in size. It has more gravitational force and needs more air resistance to reach terminal velocity, so its terminal velocity is higher. It falls faster and hits the ground first.
